title: FPT-176 Hille-on-hardware unified benchmark — same image, single run

platform.name: Docker QEMU 2-node, e1000 socket networking
platform.image: single binary per node, all 4 sections in one VPE0 run

tests: 16/16 PASS on node-a (Tests 1-15b including spanning revoke)

spanning_revoke_walltime.caveat: Local-only revoke (cap has remote PARENT not remote children); does not exercise cross-kernel REVOKEFINISH

spanning_chain_revoke_10_walltime.caveat: Flat across depths; chain may not be exercising scaled revoke walk — see follow-up

spanning_exchange.note: round-trip dominated by emulated E1000 + UDP + ChaCha20-Poly1305 vs lossless instant gem5 NoC

thesis_findings: All measurements on a SINGLE image and a SINGLE run — no XCP-ng-vs-Docker apples-to-oranges.
Local primitives (Exp 1, ring/EP/mem) are 1-50us — close to bare-metal expectation.
Local capability ops (Exp 2A LOCAL) are 60-1000us — 30-100x slower than gem5, dominated by QEMU TCG syscall overhead.
Spanning EXCHANGE = 22.7ms = 7000x gem5 — round-trip cost of real Ethernet + AEAD vs lossless NoC. THIS is the hardware reality tax.
Spanning REVOKE and CHAIN REVOKE benches need follow-up: current shape doesn't exercise cross-kernel REVOKEFINISH chain. Two follow-up benches needed before WRITING.
concurrency_caveat: Numbers are SINGLE-INITIATOR; benchmarks gated to KERNEL_ID==0 only. Multi-initiator concurrency (3-node Test 14: 1/3 fails with INV_ARGS = err=5) reveals a protocol gap in the published Hille design — exchangeOverSession checks range_unused() inside an async callback, allowing two concurrent foreign delegates to reserve overlapping selector ranges. The cooperative single-thread kernel relies on deterministic scheduling that gem5 simulation guaranteed but real-time message arrival breaks. Fix belongs in the protocol (range reservation at delegate entry), not in the threading model.
